data.title: [merge-to-main.sh]: Running merge-to-main with SIGURG interrupt then resume creates duplicate merge commit, blocking push
data.description:
## Summary
When merge-to-main.sh is killed by SIGURG (exit 144) mid-execution and then resumed with --resume, a second merge commit is created locally even though the first run may have already successfully merged and pushed to origin/main. This causes the push phase to fail with 'non-fast-forward' because the local HEAD has a different merge commit SHA than origin/main.

## Reproduction
1. Run `.claude/scripts/dso merge-to-main.sh` — it gets killed by SIGURG (exit 144)
2. Run `.claude/scripts/dso merge-to-main.sh --resume`
3. If the first run completed through the push phase, the second run creates a new merge commit (with a fresh version bump) and fails to push because origin/main is already ahead

## What was being merged
Worktree: worktree-20260412-194640
Epic: 4ba1-759f (Simplify-first: scale inference and complexity gates)
Batch commits: batches 1-5, version bump 1.12.11 -> 1.12.12
First merge commit landed successfully on origin/main as a3cf9a97

## Expected Behavior
`--resume` should detect that the push phase already completed (origin/main contains the expected commits) and skip rather than re-executing the merge/version_bump phases.

## Actual Behavior
A second merge commit (a8b6300b) is created with a duplicate version bump (also 1.12.12 but different SHA), causing push to fail with 'non-fast-forward'
